perturbation.original_text: 8. The RECIPIENT agrees to comply fully with all relevant export laws of the United Kingdom to assure that neither the Information nor any direct product thereof are (i) exported, directly or indirectly, in violation of Export Laws; or (ii) are intended to be used for any purposes prohibited by the Export Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. ONS makes no warranty regarding the accuracy or completeness of the Information provided hereunder, an d accepts no responsibility for any expenses, losses or action incurred or undertaken by the RECIPIENT as a result of the receipt or use of such Information. 8. The RECIPIENT agrees to comply fully with all relevant export laws of the United Kingdom to assure that neither the Information nor any direct product thereof are (i) exported, directly or indirectly, in violation of Export Laws; or (ii) are intended to be used for any purposes prohibited by the Export Laws.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the export compliance clause to a section dealing with warranties and responsibilities obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed, which can lead to unintentional violations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Control Reform Act mandates strict adherence to export regulations. By burying the compliance clause within unrelated contractual terms, the agreement de-emphasizes the critical nature of export law adherence, potentially increasing the risk of non-compliance, which conflicts with the intent and requirements of this Act.

Definitions In this chapter: (1) Controlled The term "controlled" refers to an item subject to the jurisdiction of the United States under subchapter I. (2) Dual-use The term "dual-use", with respect to an item, means the item has civilian applications and military, terrorism, weapons of mass destruction, or law-enforcement-related applications. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The altered text buries the export compliance clause within a section concerning warranties and responsibilities, obscuring its importance. This constitutes a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction. While neither snippet explicitly addresses clause placement, the Law Explanation indicates that this placement undermines the Export Control Reform Act by de-emphasizing adherence to export law, increasing the risk of non-compliance. This constitutes a structural flaw, per the prompt's definition: 'Structural flaws occur when the organization of a contract affects its clarity or enforceability. A legal contradiction in this category arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret.'
